body_txt: In most cases, it's hard for the average observer to guess at a character's occupation just by looking at them, unless they're actually dressed for work. "Don't judge a book by its cover" may be a bit of a cliche, but it exists for a reason: for many employees, there are no explicit tells that give away their current line of work... ...unless you know what you're looking for. In fiction and reality, some careers leave a character with subtle hints as to their current or previous employment, usually only visible to the trained eye: maybe there's some kind of habit or quirk of personality that gives away special training; maybe the job left scars of some kind, maybe even some kind of disfigurement or Distinguishing Mark . If the career is shameful or criminal in some way, the character might try to disguise the traits attached to it – only to end up accidentally revealing it at a pivotal moment. One way or another, this character has a trait that makes their job obvious to people who are either paying special attention or "in the know". In more comedic portrayals, expect the analysis to be completely wrong and the trait to have absolutely nothing to do with the character's line of work. A subtrope of Sherlock Scan and a supertrope to Can Always Spot a Cop . Compare and contrast Busman's Vocabulary , Revealing Skill , Cover-Blowing Superpower , Fighting Fingerprint , Signature Scent , Storyboard Body , and Something Only They Would Say .
